narration_segments.text: That feeling has a name. Psychology calls it imposter syndrome. Miki calls it the gap — the distance between where the body sits and where the nervous system believes it belongs. The body got the job. The nervous system is still standing at the door, waiting for someone to check its credentials and shake their head and say, sorry, there has been a mix-up, you need to leave. That gap lives in the body. Not in the mind.

Kenji strokes the cat. His shoulders drop half an inch. The breath moves one notch lower, dipping below the collarbones and touching the upper edge of the ribcage. This is amae in its simplest form. A warm body against a warm body. No words. No logic. No performance review. No evidence of worthiness presented and evaluated and stamped with approval. Just contact. Just warmth. Just the nervous system registering the presence of another living thing and softening — fractionally, but genuinely — in response. Miki teaches that imposter syndrome is not a thinking problem. It is a connection problem. The person does not lack evidence of their competence. They have evidence. They are drowning in evidence. What they lack is the felt experience of being received — fully, bodily, without condition — in the place where they now stand. Their credentials arrived. Their nervous system did not. Kenji has the skills. He has the track record. He has the respect of his team and the confidence of his manager and the proof, documented in pull requests and deployment logs and performance reviews, that he is exactly the person the company believes him to be.

With each breath, the hand delivers a message. Not a verbal message. A somatic one. A body-to-body transmission that says: I am here. I feel this. I am not running. I am not trying to fix this. I am staying. This is amae directed inward. This is the listener becoming their own source of tender reception. Not because external connection does not matter — it matters enormously, it matters biologically, it matters the way oxygen matters — but because the first act of belonging is internal. The listener belongs to themselves before they belong to any room, any company, any relationship, any title. The hand on the body is the listener claiming themselves. Receiving themselves. Saying, with touch rather than words: I accept delivery of this life, this body, this particular collection of fears and gifts and unfinished edges. I accept all of it.

Now the permission. The listener is allowed to need people. This is not weakness. It is not regression. It is not a failure of independence. It is accurate biology. The human body regulates itself through contact with other human bodies — through eye contact, through touch, through the sound of a familiar voice saying the listener's name, through the simple act of being in a room with someone who sees the listener and does not look away. The nervous system does not self-regulate in isolation. It co-regulates. It always has.
